title: How The Internet Actually Works
subtitle: DNS, TCP/IP, ports, and the handshake that starts everything

sections.title: DNS — The Phonebook of the Internet
sections.text:
When you type google.com in your browser, your computer doesn't know what that means. Computers only understand numbers — IP addresses like 142.250.80.46. So your computer asks a DNS server: "Hey, what's the IP for google.com?"

Think of it like this: you know your friend's name, but you need their phone number to call them. DNS is the contact list. The whole thing takes 10-50ms.

The flow: You type google.com → Your computer asks DNS → DNS says "that's 142.250.80.46" → Your computer connects to that IP address.
sections.caption: DNS resolution translates human-readable domains into machine-readable IP addresses in milliseconds
sections.detail: The DNS hierarchy has 13 root server clusters (letters A-M). They don't store domain records — they only know where TLD servers are. TLD servers point to authoritative nameservers that have the actual DNS records (A, AAAA, CNAME, MX, TXT, etc.). DNS results are cached at every level: your browser (60s), OS (configurable), resolver (TTL-dependent), and each nameserver. This is why changing DNS records takes time to propagate.
sections.callout: DNS resolves domain names to IPs in 10-50ms cached, 50-200ms uncached. The entire system handles trillions of queries per day.

sections.title: Ports: The Apartment Number
sections.text:
If an IP address is a building's street address, a port is the apartment number. Port 80 is for regular web traffic (HTTP), port 443 for secure traffic (HTTPS). Your database listens on its own ports too — 5432 for PostgreSQL, 3306 for MySQL.

An IP address + port together form a socket — the complete destination for your data. Think of it like mailing a letter: the IP is the street address, the port is the apartment number.
sections.caption: IP = building address, Port = apartment number. Together they form a socket.
sections.detail: Ports are divided into three ranges: well-known ports (0-1023) require admin privileges to bind — HTTP (80), HTTPS (443), SSH (22), FTP (21). Registered ports (1024-49151) are assigned by IANA for specific services like PostgreSQL (5432) and MySQL (3306). Dynamic ports (49152-65535) are used for temporary client connections. A socket is uniquely identified by the tuple (src_ip, src_port, dst_ip, dst_port, protocol).

sections.title: TCP Three-Way Handshake
sections.text:
TCP is like a phone call — before you start talking, you make sure the other person is ready. The three-way handshake establishes a reliable connection with three messages:

  1. SYN — "Can we talk?" Client sends a request to connect.
  2. SYN-ACK — "Yes, I'm ready." Server acknowledges and agrees.
  3. ACK — "Connection established!" Client confirms.

Now data can flow. Every TCP connection starts with this handshake. It takes under 100ms.
sections.caption: SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK: Three messages establish a reliable TCP connection
sections.detail: The TCP three-way handshake establishes a reliable connection before any data is sent. SYN (synchronize) initiates the connection with a random sequence number. SYN-ACK acknowledges receipt and sends the server's own sequence number. ACK confirms both sides are ready. After this, data transfer begins with sequence numbers ensuring ordered delivery. HTTP/2 and HTTP/3 optimize this with connection multiplexing and 0-RTT handshakes.
